blocks.text: JavaScript is the programming language of the Web. It is a lightweight, interpreted, or just-in-time compiled programming language with first-class functions.

items: Making web pages interactive and dynamic
Building web applications (front-end and back-end with Node.js)
It is the only language that runs natively in browsers
It is one of the most popular languages in the world
Versatile — works on frontend, backend, mobile, and desktop
Active community and constant evolution (ES6, ES7, ES2023+)

blocks.text: JavaScript runs directly in the browser's JavaScript engine (like V8 in Chrome or SpiderMonkey in Firefox). There is no compilation step needed — you write the code and the browser runs it.

rows: Replaced by V8 in modern Edge

blocks.text: Tip: Open your browser's developer tools (press F12) and go to the Console tab to run JavaScript live.
JavaScript can be placed inside HTML in three ways:

blocks.code:
<!-- 1. Inline (inside an element) -->
<button onclick="alert('Clicked!')">Click Me</button>

<!-- 2. Internal (inside a script tag) -->
<script>
  console.log("Internal script");
</script>

<!-- 3. External (best practice) -->
<script src="script.js"></script>

blocks.text: Best Practice: Always use external scripts. Place the <script> tag just before </body> for best performance.
JavaScript runs anywhere — in the browser, on the server with Node.js, or even inside desktop apps. You don't need to install anything to get started.
The fastest way to try JavaScript is your browser's built-in console.
Add JavaScript to any HTML page with a <script> tag.

blocks.code: Hello

// script.js
document.getElementById("title").textContent = "Hello from JavaScript!";

blocks.text: Node.js lets you run JavaScript outside the browser — on your computer or a server.
JavaScript syntax is the set of rules that define how a JavaScript program is written and interpreted. Understanding the basics prevents hard-to-find bugs.
JavaScript programs are made up of statements. A semicolon marks the end of a statement. JavaScript has Automatic Semicolon Insertion (ASI) — but relying on it can cause bugs.
An identifier is a name for a variable, function, or class.
Tip: Use descriptive names. getUserById is better than g or func1.
JavaScript can display output in several ways depending on where and how you want to show the data.
Avoid dialog boxes in production. They block the browser, can't be styled, and are disabled in some environments. Use custom UI instead.
Comments are lines of code that JavaScript will not execute. They explain the code to other developers — and to your future self.
JSDoc is a documentation standard for JavaScript. IDEs like VS Code use these comments to show inline hints and type information.
Good comments: Explain why something is done, not what the code does. Well-named variables and functions should already tell you what. Comments that repeat what the code says are noise.
Variables are containers for storing data values. In modern JavaScript (ES6+), we have three ways to declare variables: var, let, and const.
JavaScript is dynamically typed — variables can hold any type of data, and the type can change at runtime.

blocks.code:
// Primitive Types
let text = "Hello";          // String
let age = 25;                 // Number
let price = 19.99;            // Number (no separate float)
let isActive = true;          // Boolean
let nothing = null;           // Null
let notDefined;               // Undefined
let id = Symbol("id");        // Symbol (ES6)
let bigNum = 9007199254740991n; // BigInt (ES2020)

// Reference Types
let arr = [1, 2, 3];          // Array
let obj = { name: "Bob" };    // Object
let fn = () => {};            // Function

console.log(typeof text);     // "string"
console.log(typeof age);      // "number"
console.log(typeof isActive); // "boolean"
console.log(typeof nothing);  // "object" (historical quirk!)
console.log(typeof notDefined); // "undefined"

blocks.text: Quirk: typeof null returns "object" — this is a long-standing bug in JavaScript kept for backward compatibility.

items: Must start with a letter, underscore (_), or dollar sign ($)
Can contain letters, digits, underscores, and dollar signs
Cannot use reserved keywords (let, const, return, etc.)

blocks.text: Operators are used to perform operations on variables and values. JavaScript has several types of operators.

blocks.code:
let a = 10, b = 3;
console.log(a + b);   // 13
console.log(a - b);   // 7
console.log(a * b);   // 30
console.log(a / b);   // 3.3333...
console.log(a % b);   // 1
console.log(a ** b);  // 1000 (10^3)

blocks.text: JavaScript has two equality operators: == (loose) and === (strict). Always prefer ===.

blocks.code:
// Loose equality (==) — performs type coercion
console.log(5 == "5");   // true  ← string coerced to number
console.log(0 == false); // true  ← false coerced to 0

// Strict equality (===) — no type coercion
console.log(5 === "5");  // false ← different types
console.log(5 === 5);    // true

// Other comparison operators
console.log(10 > 5);     // true
console.log(10 < 5);     // false
console.log(10 >= 10);   // true
console.log(10 !== 5);   // true

blocks.text: Rule: Always use === and !== in modern JavaScript. The loose equality == has confusing edge cases.

blocks.code:
let x = 10;
x += 5;   // x = x + 5  → 15
x -= 3;   // x = x - 3  → 12
x *= 2;   // x = x * 2  → 24
x /= 4;   // x = x / 4  → 6
x **= 2;  // x = x ** 2 → 36
x ??= "default"; // only assigns if x is null/undefined

blocks.text: A boolean represents one of two values: true or false. Booleans are used in conditions, comparisons, and logical operations.

blocks.code:
let isLoggedIn = true;
let hasErrors = false;

// Boolean() converts any value to true/false
Boolean(1);          // true
Boolean(0);          // false
Boolean("hello");    // true
Boolean("");         // false
Boolean(null);       // false
Boolean(undefined);  // false
Boolean([]);         // true  ← empty array is truthy!
Boolean({});         // true  ← empty object is truthy!

blocks.text: Every value in JavaScript is either truthy or falsy when used in a boolean context (like an if condition).
Common gotcha: [] and {} are truthy even though they're empty. To check if an array is empty, use arr.length === 0, not just if (arr).
JavaScript can convert values from one type to another. This happens either explicitly (you do it intentionally) or implicitly (JavaScript does it automatically — called type coercion).

blocks.code:
// To Number
Number("42");        // 42
Number("3.14");      // 3.14
Number("");          // 0
Number(" ");         // 0
Number("hello");     // NaN
Number(true);        // 1
Number(false);       // 0
Number(null);        // 0
Number(undefined);   // NaN

parseInt("42px");    // 42  — stops at first non-digit
parseFloat("3.14em"); // 3.14
parseInt("0xFF", 16); // 255 — parse hex

// To String
String(42);          // "42"
String(true);        // "true"
String(null);        // "null"
String(undefined);   // "undefined"
(255).toString(16);  // "ff" (hex)
(255).toString(2);   // "11111111" (binary)

// To Boolean
Boolean(1);          // true
Boolean(0);          // false
Boolean("hi");       // true
Boolean("");         // false

// String + anything = string concatenation
"5" + 3;       // "53"  ← number coerced to string
"5" + true;    // "5true"
"" + null;     // "null"

// Arithmetic operators (other than +) coerce to number
"5" - 3;       // 2    ← string coerced to number
"5" * "2";     // 10
"10" / "2";    // 5
true + true;   // 2    ← booleans coerced to 1

// Comparison coercion
"5" == 5;      // true  ← == does coercion
"5" === 5;     // false ← === does NOT coerce
null == undefined; // true (special case)
null === undefined; // false

blocks.text: Rule: Prefer explicit conversion over relying on coercion. Use === instead of == to avoid surprise type coercion in comparisons.
Control flow determines the order in which statements are executed. JavaScript provides conditional statements and loops to control program flow.
Don't forget break! Without it, execution falls through to the next case.
Functions are reusable blocks of code designed to perform a particular task. JavaScript supports multiple function syntaxes and powerful features like closures and higher-order functions.
A closure is a function that remembers its outer scope even after that scope has finished executing.
Arrow functions don't have their own this — they inherit this from the enclosing scope. This makes them ideal for callbacks but not for object methods.
Scope determines where a variable is accessible in your code. JavaScript has three levels of scope.
Key rule: Use const and let — they respect block scope. var ignores blocks and leaks into the enclosing function.
Hoisting is JavaScript's behaviour of moving declarations to the top of their scope before code executes. What gets hoisted — and how — depends on var vs let/const vs functions.
An object is a collection of key-value pairs (properties). Objects are the fundamental building blocks of JavaScript — almost everything is an object.
Arrays are ordered lists of values. In JavaScript, arrays can hold mixed types and have a rich set of built-in methods.

blocks.code:
const arr = [1, 2, 3];

arr.push(4);       // add to end    → [1, 2, 3, 4]
arr.pop();         // remove from end → [1, 2, 3]
arr.unshift(0);    // add to start  → [0, 1, 2, 3]
arr.shift();       // remove from start → [1, 2, 3]
arr.splice(1, 1);  // remove 1 element at index 1 → [1, 3]
arr.reverse();     // reverse in place → [3, 1]
arr.sort();        // sort in place (alphabetical by default)

blocks.text: Prefer non-mutating methods in modern JavaScript. They make code easier to reason about and work well with functional programming patterns.
Strings are sequences of characters. JavaScript strings are immutable — string methods always return a new string.

blocks.code:
const str = "  Hello, World!  ";

// Case
str.toUpperCase();      // "  HELLO, WORLD!  "
str.toLowerCase();      // "  hello, world!  "

// Trimming
str.trim();             // "Hello, World!"
str.trimStart();        // "Hello, World!  "
str.trimEnd();          // "  Hello, World!"

// Searching
str.includes("World");  // true
str.startsWith("  He"); // true
str.endsWith("  ");     // true
str.indexOf("o");       // 5
str.lastIndexOf("o");   // 9

// Extracting
str.slice(2, 7);         // "Hello"
str.substring(2, 7);     // "Hello"
str.at(-3);              // "!"

// Replacing
str.replace("World", "JS");         // replaces first match
str.replaceAll("l", "L");           // replaces all
"a-b-c".split("-");                 // ["a", "b", "c"]

// Padding and Repeating
"5".padStart(3, "0");  // "005"
"ha".repeat(3);         // "hahaha"

blocks.text: JavaScript has a single Number type based on the IEEE 754 double-precision floating-point standard. This means integers and floats are the same type.

blocks.code:
const n = 3.14159;
n.toFixed(2);        // "3.14" (string)
n.toPrecision(4);    // "3.142" (string)
n.toString();        // "3.14159"
n.toString(2);       // convert to binary string

// Converting to number
Number("42");        // 42
Number("3.14");      // 3.14
Number("");          // 0
Number("abc");       // NaN
Number(true);        // 1
Number(false);       // 0
Number(null);        // 0
Number(undefined);   // NaN

parseInt("42px");    // 42 (stops at non-digit)
parseFloat("3.14kg"); // 3.14
parseInt("10", 2);   // 2 (parse binary string)

blocks.text: The Math object provides mathematical constants and functions. It is not a constructor — all properties and methods are static.

blocks.code:
// Rounding
Math.round(4.5);   // 5 (rounds to nearest)
Math.ceil(4.1);    // 5 (always rounds up)
Math.floor(4.9);   // 4 (always rounds down)
Math.trunc(4.9);   // 4 (removes decimal part)

// Absolute value
Math.abs(-5);      // 5

// Power & Root
Math.pow(2, 8);    // 256
Math.sqrt(16);     // 4
Math.cbrt(27);     // 3 (cube root)

// Min / Max
Math.min(1, 2, 3);   // 1
Math.max(1, 2, 3);   // 3
Math.min(...[5, 2, 8, 1]); // 1 (use spread for arrays)

// Logarithm
Math.log(Math.E);  // 1
Math.log2(8);      // 3
Math.log10(1000);  // 3

// Trigonometry (angles in radians)
Math.sin(Math.PI / 2);  // 1
Math.cos(0);             // 1

blocks.text: The Date object is used to work with dates and times. Internally it stores time as the number of milliseconds since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 UTC (Unix epoch).

blocks.code:
const now = new Date();                    // current date & time
const specific = new Date("2024-01-15");   // from ISO string
const fromParts = new Date(2024, 0, 15);   // year, month(0-indexed), day
const fromMs = new Date(0);               // Unix epoch: Jan 1, 1970

console.log(Date.now()); // current time as milliseconds

const d = new Date();

d.getFullYear();   // 2024
d.getMonth();      // 0-11 (0 = January!)
d.getDate();       // 1-31 (day of month)
d.getDay();        // 0-6 (0 = Sunday)
d.getHours();      // 0-23
d.getMinutes();    // 0-59
d.getSeconds();    // 0-59
d.getMilliseconds(); // 0-999
d.getTime();       // milliseconds since epoch

blocks.text: Classes (introduced in ES6) are syntactic sugar over JavaScript's prototype-based inheritance. They provide a cleaner syntax for creating objects and handling inheritance.
this refers to the object that is currently executing the function. Its value depends entirely on how the function is called — not where it is defined (except for arrow functions).

rows: Borrow a method, args as array

blocks.text: The DOM is a tree-like representation of an HTML document. JavaScript can read and modify the DOM to dynamically update the page content, structure, and styles.
Security: Never use innerHTML with untrusted user data — it opens XSS attack vectors. Use textContent for plain text.
Events are things that happen in the browser — a user clicks a button, moves the mouse, presses a key, or the page finishes loading. JavaScript lets you react to these events.
ES6 (ECMAScript 2015) was a major update to JavaScript. Since then, new features are released every year. Here are the most important ones you'll use daily.
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data-interchange format. It is easy for humans to read and write, and easy for machines to parse. JSON is the standard format for web APIs.
JSON limitations: Functions, undefined, Symbol, and circular references are not supported in JSON and will be dropped or cause an error during stringification.
Errors are inevitable. Good error handling prevents your app from crashing and gives users meaningful feedback.
Regular expressions are patterns used to match character combinations in strings. They are powerful for validation, searching, and replacing.

rows: Any of a, b, or c

blocks.text: A Map is a collection of key-value pairs where keys can be any type (not just strings). Maps maintain insertion order and are iterable.
A Set is a collection of unique values. It automatically removes duplicates and maintains insertion order.
ES2025 introduces native Set.prototype.union(), intersection(), and difference() methods — no manual implementation needed in modern environments.
JavaScript is single-threaded but handles async operations (network requests, timers, file I/O) using an event loop. Promises and async/await are the modern tools for writing async code.
Best practice: Use Promise.allSettled when you want all results regardless of failures. Use Promise.all when all must succeed.
The Fetch API provides a modern, promise-based way to make HTTP requests in the browser. It replaced the older XMLHttpRequest (XHR).
Debugging is the process of finding and fixing errors in your code. JavaScript has powerful built-in tools and browser DevTools to help.
Remember: Remove debugger statements before committing code. They have no effect if DevTools is closed.

rows: TypeError: x is not a function
TypeError: Cannot read properties of null
Check for missing brackets or commas
Add a base case to recursion

blocks.text: Browsers expose many built-in APIs beyond the DOM. These let you work with storage, geolocation, notifications, and much more.
Web Workers let you run JavaScript in a background thread, keeping the main thread free for UI rendering. Use them for CPU-intensive tasks like data processing.
